answers.answer: B) This is sunk cost fallacy - the 4 months are spent regardless. The decision should compare 3 months more vs. 2 weeks with Elasticsearch, ignoring past investment. Reason: This is classic sunk cost fallacy. The 4 months already spent cannot be recovered regardless of the decision. Correct decision framework: compare future costs (3 more months vs 2 weeks). Continuing 3 months has risk: may still be inferior to mature solution. Elasticsearch is proven, completes in 2 weeks.

Answers with confidence levels:

1. PostgreSQL default port? 5432 - Confidence: HIGH (daily use, fundamental knowledge)
2. Default max connections? 100 - Confidence: MEDIUM (recall 100, but may vary by version)
3. Theoretical max table size? 32 TB - Confidence: LOW (uncertain, could be 16TB or 32TB, need docs)
4. JSONB introduced? PostgreSQL 9.4 (2014) - Confidence: MEDIUM (recall 9.x, 9.4 is inference)
5. Fortune 500 using PostgreSQL percentage? Unknown, estimate >50% - Confidence: LOW (market data, no确切 source)
6. work_mem default in PG 16? 4 MB - Confidence: LOW (traditional default is 4MB, but new version may change)
7. Original author? Michael Stonebraker (POSTGRES project, 1986) - Confidence: HIGH (database history knowledge)
8. JSONB vs JSON performance (read-heavy)? JSONB faster - binary format, supports indexing; JSON is text format requiring parsing - Confidence: HIGH (core feature difference, hands-on experience)

REFLECTION: Least certain about specific numeric values (max table size, default config params, market data). These topics are harder because: rarely used (easy to forget), may change across versions, require official documentation verification. Most certain about conceptual knowledge and usage experience (port, JSONB advantages, history) because: daily use, principle-based knowledge is stable, backed by practical experience.
